cards.front: What are the two main memory segments used for variable allocation in C++?
cards.back: The stack (for automatic variables) and the heap (for dynamic memory allocation).

cards.text: The stack stores {{c1::local variables}}, {{c2::function parameters}}, and {{c3::function return addresses}} for each function call.

cards.front: How does the stack grow and shrink?
cards.back: The stack grows when a function is called (allocating space for local variables) and shrinks when the function returns (deallocating that space automatically).

cards.text: Stack allocation and deallocation is very {{c1::fast}} because it only requires moving the {{c2::stack pointer}}.

cards.front: What are two main limitations of the stack?
cards.back:
• Stack size is limited (typically 1-8 MB)
  • Variables are automatically destroyed when they go out of scope

A stack overflow occurs when the stack runs out of memory, typically from:
  • Too many function calls (deep recursion)
  • Allocating large local variables

cards.text: Dynamic memory allocated with {{c1::new}} or {{c2::malloc}} comes from the heap.

cards.front: What are the main advantages of heap allocation?
cards.back:
• Much larger than the stack (limited only by system memory)
  • Memory persists until explicitly deallocated
  • Can allocate memory whose size is determined at runtime

cards.front: What are the main disadvantages of heap allocation?
cards.back:
• Slower than stack allocation
  • Must be manually deallocated (risk of memory leaks)
  • Can cause fragmentation
  • Accessing heap memory is slower than stack memory

cards.question: Which variables are on the stack and which are on the heap?
cards.code:
int main()
{
    int x = 5;        // ?
    int* ptr = new int(10); // ?
    delete ptr;
    return 0;
}
cards.answer:
x is on the stack (automatic variable)
ptr is on the stack (the pointer variable itself)
The int(10) is on the heap (dynamically allocated)
cards.explanation: Local variables are on the stack. The pointer variable ptr is on the stack, but the memory it points to (allocated with new) is on the heap.

cards.concept: When to use stack vs heap allocation
cards.explanation:
Use the stack when:
  • Size is known at compile time
  • Size is small (not megabytes)
  • Variable lifetime should match scope
Use the heap when:
  • Size must be determined at runtime
  • Large allocations needed
  • Memory needs to persist beyond function scope
  • Returning objects from functions
examples: int arr[100]; // Stack: size known, small
int* arr = new int[n]; // Heap: size runtime, could be large

cards.text: Heap {{c1::fragmentation}} occurs when memory becomes divided into small non-contiguous blocks, making it hard to allocate {{c2::large contiguous}} blocks even when total free memory is sufficient.

cards.front: Why is stack allocation faster than heap allocation?
cards.back: Stack allocation only requires moving the stack pointer (one CPU instruction), while heap allocation requires searching for a free block, bookkeeping, and potential fragmentation handling.

cards.text: Stack variables are automatically destroyed when they go {{c1::out of scope}}, while heap variables must be {{c2::manually deleted}} or managed by smart pointers.
